content:
# Exodus Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~31v–32r (PDF pp. 61–62); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 41–42 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_001_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_001_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_001_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_001_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And these [are] the names of the sons of Yisra'el who came to Mitsrayim; with Ya'aqov, each-man and his house, they came:

**2** Re'uven, Shim'on, Levi, and Yehudah,

**3** Yissakhar, Zevulun, and Binyamin,

**4** Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

**5** And all the souls going-out-of the thigh of Ya'aqov were seventy souls; and Yosef was [already] in Mitsrayim.

**6** And Yosef died, and all his brothers, and all that generation.

**7** And the sons of Yisra'el were fruitful and swarmed and multiplied and became-exceedingly-exceedingly mighty; and the land was filled with them.

**8** And a new king arose over Mitsrayim who did not know Yosef.

**9** And he said to his people: "Behold, the people of the sons of Yisra'el [are] more-numerous and mightier than we.

**10** Come-now, let us deal-wisely with him, lest he multiply, and it shall be, when war befalls, that he also shall be added to those-hating-us and shall fight against us and shall go up from the land."

**11** And they set over him chiefs-of-labor-gangs in order to afflict him with their burdens; and he built store-cities for Par'oh: Pitom and Ra'amses.

**12** And as-much-as they afflicted him, so he multiplied and so he spread-out; and they felt-dread because of the sons of Yisra'el.

**13** And Mitsrayim made the sons of Yisra'el serve with crushing-harshness.

**14** And they made their lives bitter with hard labor, with mortar and with bricks and with all labor in the field -- all their labor which they made-them-labor with crushing-harshness.

**15** And the king of Mitsrayim said to the Hebrew midwives, of whom the name of the first [was] Shiphrah and the name of the second [was] Pu'ah,

**16** and he said: "When you serve-as-midwife to the Hebrew women and you look upon the birth-stones: if he [is] a son, then you shall kill him; and if she [is] a daughter, then she shall live."

**17** But the midwives feared God and did not do as the king of Mitsrayim spoke to them; and they kept-alive the male-children.

**18** And the king of Mitsrayim called to the midwives and said to them: "Why have you done this thing and kept-alive the male-children?"

**19** And the midwives said to Par'oh: "Because the Hebrew women [are] not like the Mitsri women, for they [are] vigorous -- before the midwife comes to them, they have given-birth."

**20** And God dealt-well with the midwives; and the people multiplied and became very mighty.

**21** And it was, because the midwives feared God, that he made for them houses.

**22** And Par'oh commanded all his people, saying: "Every son who is born -- into the Ye'or you shall cast him; and every daughter you shall let-live."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A)

1. **v.1 -- Shemot:** The book title in the Leningrad Codex derives from the opening word ve'eleh shemot ("and these [are] the names"). The parashah break and the decorated heading are clearly visible in the middle column of folio ~31v, following the conclusion of Genesis 50. The Masoretic section marker is present.

2. **v.5 -- "seventy souls":** The MT reads shiv'im ("seventy"). The LXX (Vaticanus, visible on p. 41) reads pente kai hebdomekonta ("seventy-five"), harmonizing with Genesis 46:27 LXX, which counts additional descendants of Yosef born in Egypt. The EMET translation follows the Hebrew MT reading of the Leningrad Codex. Stephen's speech in Acts 7:14 follows the LXX count of seventy-five.

3. **v.5 -- "going-out-of the thigh" (yots'ei yerekh):** Literally "those going out of the thigh [of Ya'aqov]." The idiom refers to direct biological descent. I have preserved the literal Hebrew construction. The LXX preserves the same anatomical idiom (ek tou merou Iakob).

4. **v.7 -- va-yishretsu:** "and they swarmed." This verb (sh-r-ts) is the same used in Genesis 1:20 for the swarming of sea creatures -- a deliberate echo of creation language applied to Israel's prolific multiplication. The LXX uses eplethuthesan, losing the creation-allusion.

5. **v.7 -- bim'od m'od:** Literally "in exceedingly exceedingly." The doubled adverb is emphatic in Hebrew. I render it "exceedingly-exceedingly" to preserve the doubling visible in the codex.

6. **v.10 -- Singular/plural shift:** The Hebrew shifts between singular ("him," referring to the people of Israel treated as a collective) and plural context throughout vv. 10-14. This is characteristic of Hebrew's treatment of 'am (people/nation) as a grammatical singular. I have preserved this shift rather than smoothing it.

7. **v.11 -- sarei missim:** Literally "chiefs of labor-gangs" or "masters of tributes/corvee." The term mas refers to conscripted forced labor. The LXX renders epistatas ton ergon, "overseers of the works."

8. **v.11 -- Pitom ve-Ra'amses:** The store-cities. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads ten te Pithom kai Ramesse and adds a third city: kai On he estin Heliou polis ("and On, which is Heliopolis"). This addition is absent from the MT and from the Leningrad Codex.

9. **v.12 -- va-yaqutsu:** "they felt dread" or "they were in anguish." The root q-w-ts carries the sense of loathing and dread combined. The LXX uses ebdelussonto ("they abhorred"), emphasizing disgust.

10. **v.14 -- be-farekh:** "with crushing-harshness." This term (parekh) appears almost exclusively in the Exodus slavery narrative. It connotes ruthless, back-breaking oppression. The LXX renders bia ("by force/violence").

11. **v.15 -- "Hebrew midwives" (ha-meyaldot ha-'ivriyot):** The phrase is ambiguous: it may mean "the midwives of the Hebrews" (i.e., Egyptian women assigned to Hebrew births) or "the Hebrew midwives" (i.e., Hebrew women who were midwives). The MT syntax and the LXX both permit either reading. Their names Shiphrah and Pu'ah appear Semitic, suggesting they were themselves Hebrew.

12. **v.16 -- ha-ovnayim:** "the birth-stones" -- a dual form, referring to the two stones or the birthing-stool upon which women crouched during delivery. Sometimes rendered "birthstool." I preserve the literal "birth-stones" as the Hebrew dual is visible in the codex. The LXX omits this detail and reads simply hotan tiktousin ("when they give birth").

13. **v.19 -- hayot:** "vigorous" or "lively." Literally "living ones" -- the midwives describe the Hebrew women as being so vital/alive that they deliver before assistance arrives. Some scholars connect this to hayyah (animal/living creature), implying animalistic vitality.

14. **v.21 -- battim:** "houses." The meaning is debated -- possibly "households/dynasties" (i.e., God established their family lines) rather than physical houses. The LXX reads epoiesan heautais oikias ("they made for themselves houses"), differing from the MT's "he [God] made for them."

15. **v.22 -- ha-ye'orah:** "into the Ye'or" -- the Nile River. Ye'or is an Egyptian loanword (from Egyptian *itrw*) used exclusively for the Nile in Hebrew scripture. I transliterate to preserve its foreign origin. The LXX reads eis ton potamon ("into the river").

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary

The Vaticanus manuscript (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 41-42) is written in continuous Greek uncial script without word divisions. The Exodus text begins on p. 41 following Genesis 50, visible in the right column. Key divergences from the MT (noted above) include: the count of seventy-five souls (v. 5), the addition of the city On/Heliopolis (v. 11), and the omission of the birth-stones detail (v. 16). The LXX text of Exodus 1 is otherwise closely aligned with the MT in content and sequence.

— EMET
